content.text: Agentic AI systems pass data through many layers: an LLM produces JSON, an orchestrator routes it, tool functions execute it, and HTTP APIs consume the results. Each hand-off is a potential breakage point when field names drift, optional keys are assumed present, or return types are misunderstood. Static type checking — the process of verifying type annotations at analysis time rather than at runtime — makes these contracts explicit and machine-verifiable, turning a class of bugs that would otherwise surface in production into errors caught locally before any code runs.
What it is

Static type checking is the analysis of Python (or TypeScript) source code against declared type annotations without executing the program. Tools such as mypy and pyright read the abstract syntax tree, infer types for every expression, and flag any location where the inferred type does not match the declared type. The analysis is purely syntactic and semantic — no data actually flows.

Python's type annotation system (PEP 484, 526, 544, 589, 604 …) lets developers attach type information to function parameters, return values, and variables. These annotations have no runtime cost by default: the interpreter stores them in __annotations__ but does not enforce them. A static checker reads those annotations offline and reports inconsistencies before the code is deployed or tested.
In an agentic context the most important type constructs are:
TypedDict
— for plain-dict shapes (tool arguments, step results, LLM payloads)
Pydantic BaseModel
— for data classes that also validate at runtime and export JSON Schema
Protocol
— for structural interfaces (the Tool contract, memory store interface)
Generic[T]
— for result wrappers, streaming iterators, and typed queues
Literal / Union / Optional
— for enumerating finite states (step status, tool names)
Why it matters

Agents are essentially distributed dataflows. The LLM outputs a JSON blob containing a tool name and argument dictionary. The orchestrator must extract that dictionary, coerce it into the right shape, and pass it to the tool implementation. If the LLM uses "query" but the tool expects "q", the call fails at runtime — and at runtime in an agent means after paying for LLM tokens, possibly after a network call to an external API, and potentially inside a long autonomous loop with no human watching.

Static type checking moves this failure to development time. Rename a field in a tool schema, and mypy immediately reports every site that still uses the old name. Add a new required parameter, and pyright flags every call site that does not supply it. This feedback loop shrinks from minutes (live test) to sub-second (editor squiggle), and from probabilistic (maybe a test covers it) to exhaustive (every code path is checked).

For agentic systems specifically, the stakes are higher than for ordinary web services: agents often take irreversible actions (delete files, send emails, submit forms). A type-unchecked plumbing error that sends the wrong argument to a destructive tool is harder to recover from than a 400 response on a read endpoint. Type discipline is a practical safety measure.
Benefits
Early error detection
— type mismatches surface in the IDE or in CI before any code runs, eliminating an entire class of runtime exceptions from production.
Living documentation
— type annotations tell every reader exactly what shape a function expects and what it returns, without maintaining a separate README.
Safe refactoring
— renaming a field, changing a return type, or adding a parameter triggers checker errors at every affected call site, making large-scale changes confident and systematic.
IDE intelligence
— Pylance (pyright in VS Code) uses the same type graph to power autocompletion, inline docs, and go-to-definition across the entire codebase.
Pydantic synergy
— Pydantic models satisfy both static checkers and runtime validators; the same model class generates the JSON Schema consumed by LLM tool definitions.
Gradual adoption
— Python's type system is opt-in. Teams can annotate core modules first and widen coverage incrementally, checking each new module with increasing strictness.
Reduced test burden
— tests that exist purely to verify that the right fields are passed can be replaced by types, freeing test budgets for behavioral, integration, and evaluation tests.
Trade-offs
LLM output is structurally untyped
— the model returns a raw string or JSON; no static checker can validate it. A runtime boundary (Pydantic parse) is always required before the typed interior can take over.
Third-party stub gaps
— some SDK packages ship incomplete or absent type stubs. Working around them requires type: ignore comments or manual stub files, which erode coverage.
Generics and Protocols have a learning curve
— correctly typing a generic streaming iterator or a Protocol with multiple type parameters takes practice, especially for developers new to typed Python.
Dynamic metaprogramming fights the checker

— decorators that alter return types, heavily dynamic dispatch, and __getattr__-based attribute access are difficult or impossible to type precisely without runtime overhead or type: ignore suppressions.
Initial annotation cost
— retrofitting a large untyped codebase requires significant upfront effort, particularly for files that mix many dynamic patterns.
Mypy vs pyright divergence
— the two major checkers occasionally disagree on edge cases. Running both is safest but doubles CI time for type checking.
How it works

When mypy or pyright analyses a file it builds a bidirectional type graph. Every expression is assigned an inferred type (derived from literals, constructor calls, and return-type annotations). Every variable reference, attribute access, and function call is checked to confirm the inferred type is assignable to the declared type at that position. Assignment compatibility follows a structural subtyping rule: a type A is compatible with type B if A exposes at least the attributes and methods that B declares with compatible types.
For generic types the checker instantiates the type parameter at each call site. A function declared as
def wrap(value: T) -> Result[T]: ...
is checked as returning Result[str] when called with a str argument. This means type errors in generic code are caught at call sites, not inside the generic implementation.

Narrowing is the mechanism by which a checker reduces the type of a variable inside conditional branches. A variable annotated as Optional[str] (i.e. str | None) narrows to str inside an if value is not None: block, so the checker accepts .upper() there but would reject it outside the guard. This makes exhaustive handling of union types enforceable.

Strictness levels control how aggressively the checker reports. At default (non-strict) level, unannotated functions are assumed to take and return Any — effectively opting them out of checking. Under --strict, every unannotated function is an error. In practice, agent code should run at least disallow_untyped_defs = true on production modules to ensure all public APIs are annotated.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the two distinct phases of type safety in an agentic system: the static analysis phase (run in CI and the IDE) and the runtime boundary phase (run every time LLM output arrives). The two phases are complementary — neither alone is sufficient.

attrs.alt: Static type checking architecture in an agentic AI system

content.text: At the top, raw LLM JSON enters the system and is immediately parsed by a Pydantic model (the boundary). A ValidationError at this point triggers a repair loop — the orchestrator sends a corrective prompt back to the LLM. A successful parse produces a typed Python object that flows into the mypy/pyright-checked orchestrator code, where all downstream code can rely on the types without further guards.

The lower half shows the static analysis CI pipeline: source files pass through the checker, which produces an error report. Developers fix errors before merge; the pipeline blocks the merge if any errors remain. The four colored boxes at the bottom show the four type-system layers used in a production agent: TypedDict for internal state bags, Pydantic models for validated I/O, Protocol for structural interfaces, and Generic[T] for typed containers.
When to use it

Apply strict static type checking to the orchestrator module, tool implementations, state management classes, and all API boundary models. These are the highest-traffic code paths and the most likely sources of silently wrong data.
Orchestrator core
— the loop that dispatches LLM output to tools is the single most important file to keep fully typed; every field access here is a potential runtime crash.
Tool function signatures
— each tool's argument model and return type should be declared explicitly; this is also the spec that generates the LLM tool definition JSON Schema.
State classes
— AgentState, ConversationHistory, MemoryEntry — all benefit from TypedDict or dataclass with slots, making field enumeration static and IDE-navigable.
API client wrappers
— thin wrappers around LLM SDKs and external APIs should annotate response shapes; use TypedDict for response dicts or Pydantic for full validation.

Where to relax: one-off evaluation scripts, Jupyter notebooks, test fixtures that deliberately construct invalid objects to test validators, and generated code (e.g. protobuf or OpenAPI clients) whose stubs may not be available or complete.

A useful decision heuristic: if a function is called from more than one place, or if its output is consumed by code you did not write, it should be typed. Single-file throwaway scripts can remain untyped.
How to implement
Step 1 — Add mypy to pyproject.toml

[tool.mypy]
python_version = "3.12"
strict = true
plugins = ["pydantic.mypy"]

# Relax only where truly needed
[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
module = ["tests.*", "scripts.*"]
disallow_untyped_defs = false

[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
module = ["some_sdk_without_stubs.*"]
ignore_missing_imports = true
Step 2 — Add pyright for editor and CI
Step 3 — Integrate into CI

# .github/workflows/type-check.yml
name: type-check
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  mypy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"
      - run: mypy src/agent_platform

  pyright:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]" pyright
      - run: pyright src/agent_platform
Step 4 — Annotate the orchestrator incrementally

Start by annotating public function signatures. mypy's --warn-return-any flag identifies functions whose return type is effectively untyped. Work top-down: annotate the entry point first, then follow the error trail into called functions.
Examples
Example 1 — TypedDict for tool arguments and orchestrator state
Example 2 — Pydantic models as the LLM boundary and tool schema source
Real-world example

A production research agent supports twelve tool functions: web search, document retrieval, SQL query, email send, calendar read, code execution, and six more. The team maintains a monorepo with mypy strict on src/ and pyright in the VS Code workspace.

When the team renamed the limit field on SearchToolArgs to max_results to align with a new external API contract, mypy caught fourteen call sites in the orchestrator, three in test helpers, and two in evaluation scripts — immediately, before any test was run. The fix took eleven minutes. Without type checking, the same rename would have silently broken any code path that specified the old field name (Pydantic would have discarded it as an unknown field under its default strict mode), producing a subtler bug: queries always used the default limit of 5 regardless of what the orchestrator requested.

The team also uses Pydantic's model_json_schema() to generate the JSON Schema object embedded in each OpenAI-style tool definition. This means the Pydantic model is the single source of truth: edit the model, and the schema the LLM sees at inference time updates automatically on the next deploy. Previously the team maintained a separate tools.json file and had repeated incidents where the LLM tool spec drifted from the actual tool implementation.
Common mistakes
Mistake 1 — Using Any as a shortcut
Mistake 2 — Skipping runtime validation of LLM output
Mistake 3 — Silent type: ignore without rationale

# Bad: suppresses the error silently; the next developer has no idea why
result = sdk.call()  # type: ignore

# Good: narrow ignore with the error code and explain the reason
result = sdk.call()  # type: ignore[no-untyped-call]  # SDK v2.3 ships no stubs
Mistake 4 — Untyped dict plumbing across module boundaries
Best practices
Validate LLM output at the entry point
— parse every response from the model into a Pydantic model before any other code touches the data. Let ValidationError propagate to a repair-prompt handler, not deep into business logic.
Use TypedDict for internal state, Pydantic for external I/O
— TypedDict has zero runtime overhead and works well for pure Python dataflows; Pydantic adds validation cost but is necessary where external data arrives.
Enable --strict incrementally
— start with disallow_untyped_defs = true, then add warn_return_any and disallow_any_generics as coverage grows, rather than trying to enable all strict flags at once on an existing codebase.
Run both mypy and pyright in CI
— they catch complementary errors; pyright is faster and integrates with VS Code via Pylance; mypy is more widely adopted in automated toolchains.
Generate JSON Schema from Pydantic models
— use model.model_json_schema() as the source of truth for LLM tool definitions. Eliminate any hand-maintained JSON Schema files.
Document every type: ignore with the error code and reason
— use # type: ignore[code]  # reason rather than bare # type: ignore. Track the count of suppressions in CI; if it grows the team is losing coverage.
Pin stub packages alongside library versions
— types-requests, types-PyYAML, etc. should be pinned in pyproject.toml [dev] dependencies so the stubs do not drift from the library.
Performance & scalability

Static type checking has no runtime cost: the checker runs offline, and Python ignores annotations at runtime unless explicitly inspected via typing.get_type_hints(). Pydantic model validation does have a small runtime cost, but Pydantic v2 (backed by the Rust pydantic-core library) benchmarks at roughly 5–50 microseconds per model parse depending on model complexity — negligible compared to LLM call latency of hundreds of milliseconds.

The mypy analysis itself scales roughly linearly with source lines but has significant fixed overhead per file (AST parsing, stub loading). For a 50 KLOC agent platform, a cold mypy run takes 30–90 seconds. Enable --cache-dir (default: .mypy_cache) so incremental runs only recheck changed files; CI typically sees 5–15 second incremental runs. Pyright is substantially faster, typically under 10 seconds even for large projects, making it more suitable for pre-commit hooks.

For large codebases with many modules, the dmypy daemon (mypy's incremental server) keeps type state in memory between runs, reducing cold-start cost. Start the daemon once in CI with dmypy start and run dmypy check src/ on subsequent steps.

# Use the mypy daemon for faster incremental checks in CI
dmypy start -- --strict --plugins pydantic.mypy
dmypy check src/agent_platform

# Or use pyright for fastest wall-clock time
pyright src/agent_platform --outputjson | jq '.generalDiagnostics | length'
Tooling & ecosystem
mypy

The reference implementation of PEP 484 checking. Mature, widely integrated, and required by many linting pipelines. Install via pip install mypy and configure in pyproject.toml under [tool.mypy]. The pydantic.mypy plugin teaches mypy about Pydantic model fields.

pip install mypy pydantic[mypy]
mypy src/agent_platform --strict
pyright / Pylance

Microsoft's type checker; runs at C++ speed via a Node binary. Pylance is the VS Code extension that embeds pyright. Pyright often catches errors mypy misses (particularly around TypeVar bounds and overloaded generics) and is generally faster. It supports reportMissingTypeStubs, reportUnknownVariableType, and a rich set of narrowing heuristics.

pip install pyright
pyright --project pyrightconfig.json src/
Pydantic

The de-facto runtime validation library for Python APIs. Pydantic v2 uses a Rust core for performance and ships with first-class mypy and pyright support. Its model_json_schema() method produces the JSON Schema object used in OpenAI-style tool definitions.

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Annotated

class RankedResult(BaseModel):
    title: str
    score: Annotated[float, Field(ge=0.0, le=1.0)]
    url: str

# mypy/pyright know that result.score is float, not object
result = RankedResult(title="foo", score=0.87, url="https://example.com")
print(result.model_json_schema())
basedpyright and beartype

basedpyright is a community fork of pyright with stricter defaults and additional checks. beartype is a runtime decorator that enforces type annotations as assertions, bridging the gap between static analysis and runtime guarantees — useful for wrapping tool functions to catch any annotation violations that Pydantic does not cover.
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Error detection time
Coverage
Runtime cost
Best for
mypy (static)
Edit / CI
All annotated code paths
None
Core orchestrator, library code
pyright (static)
Edit / CI
All annotated code paths
None
IDE feedback, fast CI
Pydantic (runtime)
First execution
Boundary inputs only
~5–50 µs per parse
LLM output, HTTP request bodies
beartype (runtime)
First execution
Every annotated call
~1–5 µs per call
Defense-in-depth on tool entry points
No typing (duck typing)
First failing execution
None
None
Throwaway scripts, notebooks
assert statements
First failing execution
Hand-specified checks only
Negligible
Spot guards; not a systematic strategy

The recommended combination is mypy strict + pyright for static analysis (catching issues at author-time) paired with Pydantic validation at runtime boundaries (catching externally sourced invalid data). beartype is optional but useful for high-assurance tool implementations where the cost is acceptable.
Security & reliability
Type discipline contributes directly to security and reliability in agentic systems:
Preventing injection via dict confusion

— if LLM output is never passed as a raw dict to a tool but always parsed into a Pydantic model first, fields that the model does not declare are silently discarded (Pydantic's default) or raise ValidationError (with extra='forbid'). This eliminates a class of prompt injection where the attacker attempts to include extra keys that the tool later uses with dict.get() with a dangerous default.
Mandatory field presence
— a required: str field in a Pydantic model cannot be absent; if the LLM omits it, ValidationError fires before any action runs. This prevents tools from acting on half-formed inputs.
Literal types for privilege escalation prevention
— using Literal["read", "list"] instead of str for a permissions field means that any value outside the allowed set is rejected at parse time, without any runtime if-statement guard.
Type narrowing catches missing None checks
— mypy's --strict-optional mode (enabled by default under strict) flags every use of an Optional value that is not guarded by a None check, preventing AttributeError on None in production.
Exhaustiveness checking for action dispatch
— using typing.assert_never() in the default branch of a Literal-typed match block makes the checker error if a new tool name is added to the Literal union without adding a corresponding handler.
Mental model

Think of static type checking as blueprint review before construction. A building blueprint specifies that the pipe labeled 'hot water supply' must connect to fittings with a 3/4-inch thread. A static checker is the inspector who reads the blueprints and flags any place where a 1/2-inch pipe is connected to a 3/4-inch fitting — before a single brick is laid.

The LLM output is raw concrete arriving from an external supplier — it might be the right mix or it might not. The Pydantic boundary is the quality-control test at the delivery gate. Everything inside the site (the typed orchestrator) can proceed with the assumption that the material meets spec, because only verified material passed the gate.
Key takeaways
Static type checking (mypy, pyright) catches field mismatches, wrong argument types, and missing return annotations before any code runs — turning runtime exceptions into edit-time or CI failures.
LLM output is structurally untyped; always parse it into a Pydantic model at the system boundary before it enters the type-checked interior of the agent.

Pydantic BaseModel serves a dual purpose: runtime validation of external data, and the single source of truth for the JSON Schema embedded in LLM tool definitions — eliminating drift between spec and implementation.
Enable --strict (mypy) or typeCheckingMode: strict (pyright) on production modules; relax strictness only for test fixtures, generated code, and evaluation scripts.
TypedDict and dataclass are the right choice for typed internal state (zero runtime cost); reserve Pydantic models for external I/O where coercion and validation are needed.

Use Literal types and typing.assert_never() in dispatch switches to make the checker enforce exhaustive handling — new tool names added to the Literal union must have a corresponding handler or the checker reports an error.
Annotate every type: ignore with its specific error code and a comment explaining why it is suppressed; treat the count of suppressions as a quality metric that should not grow.
Run the mypy daemon (dmypy) or pyright for fast incremental checks in CI and pre-commit hooks; cache results with --cache-dir to avoid redundant full rebuilds.
